title: Health Savings Account (HSA) and Health Care FSA Coordination

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must coordinate HSA contributions with Health Care FSA eligibility when changing employers or health plans, maximizing tax-advantaged healthcare savings while avoiding prohibited contribution overlaps
criteria: HSA requires enrollment in High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) with minimum deductibles and maximum out-of-pocket limits
Cannot contribute to both general purpose Health Care FSA and HSA in same year
Limited Purpose FSA (dental/vision only) or Post-Deductible FSA compatible with HSA contributions
Mid-year job change may allow HSA at new employer if old employer FSA exhausted or limited purpose
2025 HSA contribution limits: $4,300 individual, $8,550 family, plus $1,000 catch-up (age 55+)
Health Care FSA limit for 2025: $3,200 with $640 carryover permitted (or 2.5 month grace period)
Last-month rule allows full HSA contribution if enrolled December 1 and maintain HDHP for following 12 months
Prorated HSA contributions based on months of HDHP coverage unless last-month rule applies

hsa_fsa_coordination.triple_tax_benefit: HSA offers tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses
hsa_fsa_coordination.fsa_use_or_lose: Health Care FSA subject to use-or-lose rule (with carryover or grace period option) unlike HSA rollover
hsa_fsa_coordination.hsa_disqualification: General purpose Health Care FSA disqualifies HSA contributions; must be limited purpose or post-deductible
hsa_fsa_coordination.mid_year_changes: Job change or plan change mid-year requires careful coordination to avoid excess contributions and penalties
hsa_fsa_coordination.contribution_timing: HSA contributions can be made until tax filing deadline; FSA elections generally locked at annual enrollment

restrictions: Cannot contribute to HSA if enrolled in Medicare (Part A, B, C, or D) or claimed as dependent
Cannot contribute to HSA if covered by general purpose Health Care FSA (own or spouse's plan)
Cannot contribute to HSA if covered by spouse's non-HDHP that provides coverage to you
FSA run-out period (incur expenses during plan year, submit claims after) doesn't affect HSA eligibility
HSA excess contributions subject to 6% excise tax annually until corrected; must withdraw excess with earnings
Last-month rule requires maintaining HDHP for 12-month testing period; failure causes income inclusion and 10% penalty
State tax treatment varies; California and New Jersey don't recognize HSA tax benefits at state level
Health Care FSA cannot be converted to Limited Purpose FSA mid-year without qualifying event or plan design
